subject: general-histology
title: General Histology — Electron Microscope Slide ID
description: Flashcards: study each transmission electron micrograph and its numbered structures, then hit Answer to reveal them. Micrographs from the General Histology slideshow; annotations from the GH specimen notes.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of two adjacent epithelial cells; the left one (1) filled with large pale secretory vesicles, the darker right one (2) bearing apical projections.

explanation.text:
Goblet (mucous) cell + ciliated cell, trachea.
(1) Goblet / mucous cell — cytoplasm full of large, pale (electron-lucent) mucus vesicles
(2) Adjacent ciliated cell — cilia projecting from the free (apical) surface, here cut in cross-section
The pale, lucent vesicles distinguish the mucous cell from the serous cell's dense granules.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #4 · slideshow p.28
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM: goblet/mucous cell (1) beside a ciliated cell (2), free surface labelled.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of a cell packed with many round electron-dense granules (1), with rough ER (2) and a rounded nucleus (3).

explanation.text:
Serous (secretory / zymogenic) cell.
(1) Abundant, electron-DENSE secretory (zymogen) granules filling the apical cytoplasm
(2) Rough endoplasmic reticulum (basal)
(3) Prominent, rounded, euchromatic nucleus
Contrast with the mucous cell, whose secretory vesicles are pale and electron-lucent.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #5 · slideshow p.29
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a serous cell labelling secretory granules, RER, nucleus and nuclear membrane.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of a cell with a large pale nucleus (1) and mitochondria (2), surrounded by banded collagen fibrils cut lengthwise (3) and in cross-section (4).

explanation.text:
Fibroblast (active).
(1) Large, euchromatic (pale) nucleus
(2) Abundant mitochondria / organelles — a cell actively synthesising matrix
(3) Collagen fibrils in longitudinal section, showing alternating dark/light banding (procollagen periodicity)
(4) Collagen fibrils in transverse section
Collagen lies OUTSIDE the cell, embedded in ground substance. A fibrocyte is smaller with a dark, heterochromatic nucleus.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #6 · slideshow p.30
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of an active fibroblast: euchromatic nucleus, mitochondria, longitudinal and transverse collagen fibrils.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of a small cell with an elongated dark nucleus (1) amid collagen fibrils cut transversely (2), obliquely (3) and longitudinally (4).

explanation.text:
Fibrocyte (quiescent fibroblast).
(1) Elongated, heterochromatic (dark) nucleus with little surrounding cytoplasm — smaller and less active than a fibroblast
(2) Collagen fibrils in transverse section
(3) Collagen fibrils in oblique section
(4) Collagen fibrils in longitudinal section
Ground substance surrounds the extracellular collagen.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #7 · slideshow p.31
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a fibrocyte: heterochromatic nucleus with collagen fibrils in longitudinal, oblique and transverse section.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of a large cell body with a big pale nucleus (1), stacks of rough ER (2), lysosomes (3) and mitochondria (4), surrounded by fine processes (5).

explanation.text:
Neuron cell body (perikaryon).
(1) Large nucleus with nuclear envelope (arrows), typically a prominent nucleolus
(2) Abundant rough ER = Nissl substance / bodies
(3) Lysosomes
(4) Mitochondria
(5) Surrounding neuropil — the felt-work of fine neuronal and glial processes
(The plasma membrane is marked by double arrows.)
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #18 · slideshow p.32
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a neuron cell body: nucleus, RER/Nissl, lysosomes, mitochondria and surrounding neuropil.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of striated muscle showing repeating units between Z-lines, with dark A-bands, light I-bands, a central H-zone and M-line, plus mitochondria.

explanation.text:
Sarcomere — the contractile unit, Z-disc to Z-disc.
• Z-disc sits in the middle of the light I-band (thin / actin filaments only)
• A-band (dark): the full length of the thick / myosin filaments plus their overlap with actin
• H-zone: lighter central part of the A-band (myosin only); the M-line runs through its centre
• Also visible: mitochondria, part of the muscle-cell nucleus, and T-tubules (T)
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #16 · slideshow p.33
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a sarcomere labelling Z, A, I and H bands, M-line, mitochondria and nucleus.

questions.imageAlt: TEM showing a small vessel with a narrow lumen (1) lined by a continuous endothelial cell (2) wrapped by a pericyte (4); myelinated fibers nearby (5).

explanation.text:
Continuous (non-fenestrated) capillary.
(1) Capillary lumen
(2) Endothelial cell nucleus — a continuous wall with minimal permeability
(3) Collagen fibrils
(4) Pericyte cytoplasm wrapping the endothelium
(5) Myelinated nerve fibres in the surrounding CNS parenchyma
The continuous, unbroken endothelium (no fenestrae) fits the blood–brain barrier.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #12 · slideshow p.34
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a continuous CNS capillary: lumen, endothelial nucleus, pericyte and nearby myelinated fibres.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of nervous tissue: a vesicle-filled terminal (1) meeting a postsynaptic element (2) across a dense cleft (3); myelinated (4) and unmyelinated (5) fibers nearby.

explanation.text:
A chemical synapse (with nearby myelinated and unmyelinated fibres).
(1) Presynaptic axon terminal (bouton) packed with synaptic vesicles (neurotransmitter)
(2) Postsynaptic element — e.g. a dendritic spine
(3) Synaptic cleft — electron-dense
(4) Two myelinated axons (thick, dark myelin sheaths)
(5) Two unmyelinated fibres (or dendrites)
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #19 · slideshow p.35
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of a chemical synapse: pre- and post-synaptic elements, synaptic cleft and myelinated axons.

questions.imageAlt: TEM of several small axons embedded in the cytoplasm of one Schwann cell, none with a myelin sheath; microtubules and neurofilaments visible.

explanation.text:
Unmyelinated nerve fibres.
• Several axons enclosed within the cytoplasm of one non-myelinating Schwann cell (2) — no myelin sheath
• Axoplasm contains microtubules (double arrows) and neurofilaments (arrowheads)
(1) Mitochondria within the axons
Myelinated fibres, by contrast, are wrapped by thick concentric myelin.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #20 · slideshow p.36
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated TEM of unmyelinated nerve fibres: Schwann cell cytoplasm, neurofilaments, microtubules and mitochondria.
